threats.title: Missing Authentication on WebSocket Connection (lib/startup/registerWebsocketEvents.ts:24)
threats.scenario: Any internet-facing client can open a Socket.IO connection to ws://host:3000/socket.io/ without presenting any credential. The `io.on('connection', ...)` handler at line 24 applies no authentication middleware, JWT check, or session validation before processing the socket. An unauthenticated attacker can connect and (1) receive all pending `challenge solved` notifications buffered in-memory, (2) send `notification received` messages to delete others' notifications, and (3) trigger challenge-verification handlers. The Socket.IO CORS option at line 20 (`origin: 'http://localhost:4200'`) only governs XHR polling CORS headers; native WebSocket upgrades bypass this restriction entirely in browsers that support the WS protocol directly.
threats.evidence_summary: `io.on('connection', (socket: any) => { ... })` at line 24 executes all message handlers with no preceding authentication check or middleware.
threats.impact_description: Any external actor gains full access to real-time server state and all message handlers, indistinguishable from an authenticated session.
threats.controls_in_place: Socket.IO CORS option restricts `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` to localhost:4200 for XHR polling; no equivalent guard exists for native WebSocket upgrade connections.
threats.mitigation_title: Add authentication middleware to Socket.IO connection handler in registerWebsocketEvents.ts

steps: Implement a Socket.IO `io.use()` middleware that reads the JWT from the `auth` handshake parameter or `Authorization` header: `io.use((socket, next) => { const token = socket.handshake.auth.token; verify(token, secret, (err, decoded) => { if (err) return next(new Error('Unauthorized')); socket.data.user = decoded; next(); }); })`.

Reject unauthenticated connections before the `connection` event fires; ensure the client sends the Bearer token in the Socket.IO handshake `auth` object: `io('ws://host:3000', { auth: { token: localStorage.getItem('token') } })`.
Scope per-message handlers (`notification received`, `verifyLocalXssChallenge`, etc.) to check `socket.data.user` before processing, ensuring session identity is available for all handler logic.

threats.title: Unauthenticated Mutation of Shared Notification State (lib/startup/registerWebsocketEvents.ts:34)
threats.affected_parameter: data (flag value in notification received event)
threats.scenario: The `notification received` socket event handler at line 34-38 accepts a `data` value from any connected client and uses it to find and splice entries from the global in-memory `notifications` array: `const i = notifications.findIndex(({ flag }) => flag === data); if (i > -1) { notifications.splice(i, 1); }`. No authentication check precedes this write. An unauthenticated attacker who knows (or guesses) any flag string can delete that notification from the server's in-memory queue, causing legitimate users who connect afterward to never receive that challenge notification. Because the `notifications` array is module-level shared state, deletion affects all future connections.
threats.evidence_summary: `notifications.splice(i, 1)` at line 37 is reachable by any unauthenticated WebSocket client by sending a `notification received` event containing any flag value.
threats.impact_description: An attacker can silently purge challenge notifications from the server queue, denying them to all subsequent connecting users without any authentication requirement.
threats.controls_in_place: None — no authentication, no rate limit, no ownership check on the notification being deleted.
threats.mitigation_title: Require authenticated socket identity before processing notification received events in registerWebsocketEvents.ts

steps: After adding the `io.use()` authentication middleware (see realtime-channel-001), validate that `socket.data.user` owns or is entitled to dismiss the notification referenced by `data` before calling `notifications.splice()`.
Scope notification dismissal to per-user notification tracking rather than a shared global array — store notifications keyed by userId so one user's dismissal cannot affect another's queue.

threats.title: Unauthenticated Challenge Solve Bypass via WebSocket Handlers (lib/startup/registerWebsocketEvents.ts:41)
threats.scenario: Three socket event handlers — `verifyLocalXssChallenge` (line 41), `verifySvgInjectionChallenge` (line 46), and `verifyCloseNotificationsChallenge` (line 50) — accept attacker-controlled data and directly invoke `challengeUtils.solveIf()`, which writes `challenge.solved = true` to the database. An unauthenticated attacker can: (1) send `{event: 'verifyLocalXssChallenge', data: '<iframe src="javascript:alert(`xss`)">' }` to mark the XSS challenge solved without executing XSS in a browser; (2) send a path string matching the SVG injection regex to solve that challenge; (3) send `[1, 2]` (a two-element array) to solve the close-notifications challenge. In all cases `challengeUtils.solve()` persists the result to the database, bypassing the intended in-browser verification flow.
threats.evidence_summary: `challengeUtils.solveIf(challenges.localXssChallenge, () => utils.contains(data, ...))` at line 42 is callable by any unauthenticated socket client by sending the known payload string.
threats.impact_description: An unauthenticated attacker gains full credit for multiple CTF challenges without performing the required security exercises, undermining the integrity of the application's challenge system.
threats.controls_in_place: None — no authentication on connection or on the individual handler invocations.
threats.mitigation_title: Gate challenge-verification socket handlers behind authenticated socket sessions in registerWebsocketEvents.ts

steps: Implement connection-level authentication via `io.use()` middleware (see realtime-channel-001) so challenge verification handlers are only reachable by authenticated users.
Within each verification handler, additionally bind the solve action to the authenticated `socket.data.user.id` so only the user whose session performed the browser-side action receives credit.

Consider server-side verification of the actual user action context (e.g., checking that the XSS payload was reflected in a real HTTP response to the user's session) rather than accepting the payload string over WebSocket as proof of exploitation.

threats.title: Unauthenticated CTF Flag Broadcast on WebSocket Connect (lib/startup/registerWebsocketEvents.ts:30)
threats.scenario: When any client connects to the WebSocket, the handler at lines 30-32 iterates all pending entries in the `notifications` array and emits each as a `challenge solved` event. The notification object (built in challengeUtils.ts:58-66) includes the `flag` field — the CTF flag value computed by `utils.ctfFlag(challenge.name)`. An unauthenticated attacker opens a WebSocket connection and immediately receives all pending challenge flags, including flags from other users' solved challenges that haven't been acknowledged yet. No authentication, no scoping to the connecting user's identity.
threats.evidence_summary: `notifications.forEach((notification) => { socket.emit('challenge solved', notification) })` at line 30-31 unconditionally replays all pending notifications — including `flag` values — to every connecting socket regardless of identity.
threats.impact_description: An attacker obtains CTF flag values for challenges they have not solved, defeating the purpose of the Juice Shop challenge system.
threats.controls_in_place: Notification description is HTML-sanitized (challengeUtils.ts:61) before inclusion, but the flag field itself is not gated or redacted.
threats.mitigation_title: Scope challenge solved notification broadcast to the authenticated owning user's socket in challengeUtils.ts and registerWebsocketEvents.ts

steps: After adding socket authentication (realtime-channel-001), store each notification with the `userId` of the solver and only emit `challenge solved` events to sockets associated with that user via `io.to(socketId).emit(...)` rather than the global broadcast `io.emit()`.
In `registerWebsocketEvents.ts` line 30-31, filter the notifications replay to only entries whose `userId` matches `socket.data.user.id` to prevent cross-user flag leakage on connection.
Alternatively, if CTF flags should be globally visible (e.g., shared challenge board), remove the `flag` field from broadcast payloads and serve it only through an authenticated REST endpoint.

threats.title: Unbounded WebSocket Connection Acceptance Without Rate Limiting (lib/startup/registerWebsocketEvents.ts:19)
threats.scenario: The Socket.IO server created at line 19-20 accepts connections from any client with no connection rate limiting, no per-IP limit, and no maximum concurrent connection count. An attacker can open thousands of simultaneous WebSocket connections from a single host or distributed botnet. Each connection triggers the notification replay loop (lines 30-32), consuming CPU and memory proportional to the notifications array size. Socket.IO maintains per-connection state (socket object, event listener registrations) that accumulates unbounded. Combined with the absence of authentication, this attack requires no credentials.
threats.evidence_summary: `new Server(server, { cors: { origin: 'http://localhost:4200' } })` at line 20 creates the Socket.IO server with no `maxHttpBufferSize`, no connection limit, and no rate-limiting option configured.
threats.impact_description: An attacker exhausts server file descriptors, memory, and CPU, causing a denial of service for all legitimate WebSocket and HTTP users.
threats.controls_in_place: None identified — no connection limit, no rate limiting middleware.
threats.mitigation_title: Enforce connection rate limits and maximum concurrent WebSocket connections in registerWebsocketEvents.ts

steps: Configure Socket.IO `maxHttpBufferSize` option to cap incoming message size (e.g., `new Server(server, { maxHttpBufferSize: 1e4 })` for 10KB) to limit per-message memory consumption.

Implement a per-IP connection rate limiter using `socket.handshake.address` in the `io.use()` middleware: track connection counts per IP in a Map or Redis and reject if over threshold (e.g., 10 connections/IP).
Set a maximum message rate per socket using a token-bucket limiter on incoming events to prevent event flooding on existing connections.
Consider wrapping the Socket.IO endpoint behind the same Express rate-limiting middleware used for HTTP routes (e.g., `express-rate-limit` applied to `/socket.io/`).

threats.title: ReDoS via Unanchored Regex on Attacker-Controlled WebSocket Data (lib/startup/registerWebsocketEvents.ts:47)
threats.scenario: The `verifySvgInjectionChallenge` handler at line 46-47 calls `data?.match(/.*\.\.\/\.\.\/\.\.[\ w/-]*?\/redirect\?to=https?:\/\/placecats\.com\/(g\/)?[\d]+\/[\d]+.*/)` where `data` is fully attacker-controlled. The regex has unanchored `.*` at both ends and a lazy `[\ w/-]*?` in the middle. Node.js uses a backtracking regex engine. An attacker can send a crafted string such as `'../../../' + 'a/'.repeat(50000)` that causes catastrophic backtracking — the engine repeatedly tries combinations of the lazy quantifier against the fixed suffix `/redirect?to=...` that never appears. This blocks the JavaScript event loop thread for the duration of the match, causing all Socket.IO events to stall.
threats.evidence_summary: `data?.match(/.*\.\.\/\.\.\/\.\.[\ w/-]*?\/redirect\?to=.../)`  at line 47 applies an unanchored backtracking regex to an attacker-supplied string with no length or content pre-check.
threats.impact_description: A single crafted WebSocket message can block the Node.js event loop for seconds to minutes, causing a denial of service for all active connections.
threats.controls_in_place: None — no input length limit, no pre-validation before the regex match.
threats.mitigation_title: Pre-validate and length-cap data before regex match in verifySvgInjectionChallenge handler

steps: Add a length check before the regex: `if (typeof data !== 'string' || data.length > 500) return;` to reject oversized or non-string inputs.

Rewrite the regex to be anchored and non-backtracking: replace the leading `.*` with `^[^]*?` and reduce ambiguity in the middle quantifier, or use a non-backtracking approach by splitting on known delimiters first.
Consider using the Node.js `vm.runInNewContext()` with a timeout, or the `re2` package (a linear-time RE2 engine for Node), for any regex applied to untrusted input.
